title: Stock Photography Editing Philosophy for Shutterstock
derived_from: Shutterstock Content Quality Standards: Editing Techniques
Shutterstock Description and Keyword Best Practices
core_principle: The photo should look like it was taken perfectly in-camera by a skilled photographer -- not like it was rescued in post-production.

philosophy.the_golden_rule: If a viewer can tell the photo was edited, you've edited too much. Shutterstock reviewers inspect at 100% zoom. Every adjustment must be invisible at full resolution.

authenticity_over_perfection.description: The 2026 stock market has shifted decisively toward authentic imagery. Buyers across editorial, commercial, and brand campaigns are tired of the over-art-directed aesthetic. They want photos that look like they came from a real person's camera, not a studio.
principles: Natural lighting wins over studio lighting for most categories
The Instagram-feed aesthetic is now the target, not the magazine-cover aesthetic

technical_excellence_is_non_negotiable.description: While the aesthetic has moved toward authentic/casual, technical quality standards remain extremely high. Shutterstock will reject technically flawed images regardless of how interesting the subject is.
requirements: Sharp focus on the intended subject (eyes for portraits, horizon for landscapes)
Correct exposure -- no clipped highlights or crushed shadows
Clean noise profile -- invisible at 100% zoom
No compression artifacts, banding, or posterization
No dust spots, sensor marks, or lens flare (unless intentional and artistically justified)
No visible editing artifacts (halos, clone-stamp patterns, color fringing)

less_is_more.description: The most common rejection reason after technical issues is over-editing. Stock reviewers are trained to spot over-processed images.
avoid: Over-sharpening -- creates halos and crispy texture. Max amount: 30-40 for stock.
Over-saturation -- neon blues, orange sunsets, electric greens are instant flags
Over-NR (noise reduction) -- plastic/waxy skin, lost texture detail
HDR look -- local contrast pushed too hard, unnatural halo between sky and ground
Heavy vignette -- dates the image and screams 'edited'
Film grain added in post -- Shutterstock wants clean images
Extreme color grading -- teal-and-orange, cross-processing, vintage filters

stock_ready_editing_process.description: Step-by-step editing process for the Stock Ready mode. Each step should be invisible when viewed at 100%.

operations.description: Auto-detect and correct tilted horizons. Even 0.5 degrees off is noticeable.
Scan for and remove sensor dust marks. Check sky areas and uniform surfaces at 100%.
Remove purple/green fringing on high-contrast edges. Common on wide-angle lenses.
Recover clipped highlights. Lift crushed shadows. Target: histogram fully within bounds with 2% headroom on each side.
Neutralize color casts. For stock, white balance should be accurate/neutral. Slight warmth (+3 shift) is acceptable for lifestyle/food.
Slight contrast boost for visual pop. Should not create crushed blacks or blown highlights.
Boost under-saturated colors. NEVER boost already-saturated colors. Vibrance, not saturation.
Apply ONLY to smooth areas (sky, out-of-focus backgrounds). NEVER to textured areas (hair, fabric, stone, foliage).
Capture sharpening only. Applied at the very end. Must be invisible at 100% -- no halos, no crunchy edges.
Crop to nearest standard ratio if needed. Leave negative space for text placement (increases commercial value).
Flag but don't auto-remove. Distracting elements at frame edges should be cropped out. Major distractions should be flagged for manual review.
Save at quality 95. No lower -- compression artifacts will be visible.

technical_execution: Focus: Is the subject sharp? Are the eyes in focus for portraits?
Exposure: Is the histogram well-distributed? Any clipping?
Noise: Visible at 100%? Especially in shadows and sky gradients?
Artifacts: Compression blocking? Banding in gradients?
Sharpening: Halos around edges? Over-processed crispy look?
NR artifacts: Plastic/waxy skin? Lost fine detail?
composition: Is the framing intentional? Does it complement the subject?
Are there distracting elements entering the frame?
Does the image have commercial value? (negative space for text?)
metadata: Does the title describe what's in the image? (sentence, not keywords)
Are keywords relevant and precise? (7-50, no stuffing)
Are 1-2 categories selected from the approved list?
Are there identifiable people without model releases?
Are there visible trademarks or brand logos?
post_production: Is clone-stamp/healing work clean? (no repeating patterns, edge artifacts)
Is color grading natural? (no extreme casts or over-saturation)

demand_intelligence_2026.biggest_opportunity: Content that breaks the polished stock photo look. Natural lighting, imperfect compositions, real textures. Buyers want imagery that could plausibly come from a real person's camera rather than a commissioned studio shoot.

niche_strategy.principle: Don't chase the biggest categories. Find under-served sub-niches within popular categories.
niche_strategy.method: The 20-60-20 rule: 20% of your portfolio drives 80% of earnings. That 20% almost always comes from landing in a sub-niche with high demand and low supply, not from competing in saturated broad categories.

content_brief_keywords.description: Shutterstock publishes magic keywords monthly. Including these in your metadata gets your images surfaced to active buyers.

metadata_optimization.title_format: Natural sentence answering Who + What + Where. Example: 'Young woman working on laptop in cozy home office with morning sunlight'

keyword_layers.layer_1_literal: First 7-10 keywords. Physical subjects: woman, laptop, home office, desk, coffee, morning
keyword_layers.layer_2_conceptual: Next 10-15. Themes: remote work, productivity, work-life balance, freelance, independence
keyword_layers.layer_3_stylistic: Last 5-10. Visual: natural light, warm tones, shallow depth of field, lifestyle, candid
keyword_layers.layer_4_use_cases: Optional. How buyers use it: blog header, social media, website banner, presentation

metadata_optimization.critical_rule: First 7-10 keywords carry the most ranking weight in Shutterstock's AI search

briefs.name: On The Move
briefs.description: People buying homes, renting, unpacking boxes worldwide
briefs.diversity_note: Feature diverse ethnicities, ages, abilities, LGBTQ+ individuals

briefs.name: Starting a Business
briefs.description: Entrepreneurs in early stages of starting brands and businesses
examples: meeting with architects in empty office space

briefs.name: Indulge
briefs.description: People enjoying special experiences -- work-life balance

compliance_rules: Content with recognizable faces MUST include model releases
For recognizable interiors of private properties, include property release

top_selling_sub_niches_2026.description: Don't chase broad categories -- find under-served sub-niches. These are specific angles within popular categories that have high demand but low supply.
examples: Remote work from unusual locations (not just home offices)
Mental health concepts beyond meditation (therapy, support groups, journaling)
Food prep in home kitchens (not studio setups)
Electric vehicle charging in real parking lots
Solo female travelers in non-tourist settings
Casual business meetings in coffee shops
avoid_oversaturated: Standard office meeting rooms with whiteboards

shooting: Shoot in RAW -- gives maximum editing latitude without quality loss
Keep ISO as low as possible -- high ISO noise is the #1 technical rejection reason
Use natural light when possible -- buyers prefer it over studio lighting in 2026
Shoot both horizontal AND vertical of the same scene -- doubles your catalog value
Leave negative space for text/copy placement -- increases commercial value significantly
Include diverse representation -- diverse images consistently outperform homogeneous ones
Remove brands/logos from scene BEFORE shooting -- easier than cloning in post
post_production: Edits must be INVISIBLE at 100% zoom -- this is the single most important rule
Fix technical issues (dust, horizon, CA) -- these are mandatory
Gentle exposure/contrast adjustments -- stay within ±8% of original
Selective noise reduction only -- never global, never heavy
Sharpening amount 30 max for stock -- anything higher creates visible halos
Never add vignette, grain, or heavy color grading -- signals over-editing
Clone/heal cleanup must be undetectable -- no repeating patterns
what_gets_rejected: Noise/grain visible at 100% (especially shadows and gradients)
Visible dust spots or sensor marks
